common_name: Siam Queen Basil (Thai Basil, Clove Basil)

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0) soil in full sun to partial shade; in zone 9, Siam Queen can be started any time from February through August and will produce through the growing season, cutting back after flowering to stimulate fresh growth.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Siam Queen is the 1997 All-America Selections Herb Award winner — the premier Thai basil cultivar for American gardens, producing a compact, well-branched plant with deeply fragrant, dark-green, slightly crinkled leaves and a distinctive spicy-clove fragrance that is sharply different from sweet Italian basil. The clove-anise-pepper flavor profile of Siam Queen is essential to authentic Thai, Vietnamese, and other Southeast Asian cuisines — it is the correct basil for Thai basil chicken (gai pad krapao), pho, green papaya salad, and spring rolls. Unlike sweet basil cultivars, Siam Queen's flavor and fragrance remain strong even after the plant flowers, giving it a longer useful harvest window. The ornamental purple flower spikes and purple stems add visual interest beyond the herb's culinary value. In zone 9, Siam Queen grows vigorously through the long warm season and handles the summer heat better than many sweet basil cultivars — pinching the flowering stems back regularly prolongs the harvest but the herb remains usable even when flowering. The compact plant is well-suited to container growing where it can be positioned conveniently near the kitchen for ready harvesting. Excellent in containers and raised beds.

common_name: Red Rubin Basil (Purple Basil, Red Basil)

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Red Rubin holds its deep purple color best in full sun — in shade the leaves may become more greenish-purple; pinch flowering stems to maintain full-leaf production.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Red Rubin is the finest and most consistent deep-purple basil cultivar — an improved selection of 'Dark Opal' basil that produces more uniformly dark, deep burgundy-purple leaves across the entire plant with a richer, more saturated color than older purple basil selections. The deep crimson-purple foliage provides striking ornamental value in the herb garden and the edible landscape — the dark leaves contrast beautifully with green herbs, chartreuse foliage, and brightly colored flowers. Red Rubin basil has the same classic sweet basil fragrance and flavor as green Genovese-type basils and can be used interchangeably in cooking, where its purple color creates dramatic purple-tinted basil oil, purple pesto, and deep-toned herb vinegars that standard green basil cannot produce. The purple color also migrates to other ingredients when the fresh herb is used — providing a subtle tinting effect in salads. In zone 9, Red Rubin grows vigorously through the warm season. Regular harvest by pinching the upper leaves and flower buds maintains the bushy habit and delays flowering. Once the plant bolts and sets seed, leaf production diminishes — cut back hard or compost the plant and start fresh.

common_name: Gulf Coast Phlox (Trailing Phlox, Pineleaf Phlox)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in late winter or early spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or gritty soil in full sun to partial shade; excellent drainage is essential — Phlox nivalis will rot in heavy or wet soils, particularly during summer.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, sandy or gritty, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Phlox nivalis, the trailing phlox or pineleaf phlox, is a low-growing, mat-forming native phlox found naturally in the Coastal Plains and sandhills from Virginia through the Gulf Coast states — ideally adapted to the well-drained, lean, sandy pine-savanna soils of the Gulf South where most other ground covers struggle. In early spring (March–April in zone 9), the spreading mats of fine, needle-like evergreen foliage are covered with large (3/4 inch), five-petaled flowers in shades of pink, lavender-pink, or white, creating a stunning carpet of color before the heat of summer arrives. The flower display is brief but intensely colorful, and after bloom the tight, fine-textured evergreen mats remain attractive year-round as a weed-suppressing ground cover. Phlox nivalis is native to Gulf Coast longleaf pine savannas — it is particularly adapted to the combination of infertile, sandy, well-drained soil, full sun to dappled pine shade, and periodic drought that characterizes these ecosystems. Avoid heavy clay or consistently moist soil; excellent drainage is the key to success. A superb choice for rock gardens, sunny slopes, and lean sandy bank stabilization in zone 7–9.

common_name: Karl Foerster Feather Reed Grass (Upright Feather Reed Grass)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Karl Foerster is unusual among ornamental grasses in tolerating partial shade as well as heavy clay soils — both qualities expand its planting options in typical residential landscapes.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Karl Foerster Feather Reed Grass was named the Perennial Plant of the Year in 2001 and is the single most important ornamental grass in American landscape design — a sterile, non-invasive hybrid producing stiffly upright, narrow clumps of arching green foliage that in late spring through summer send up tall (4–5 feet), feathery, reddish-purple flower plumes that gradually dry to warm buff-gold and persist beautifully through winter. The four-season interest of Karl Foerster is exceptional: spring green vertical foliage, summer red-purple flower plumes, fall buff-gold dried plumes, and winter structural sculptural form — all without any invasive seeding or aggressive spreading. The narrow, upright, 2–3 foot wide clumps fit neatly into smaller garden spaces where spreading grasses would be impractical. Karl Foerster tolerates clay soil, periodic wetness, drought once established, and partial shade better than most ornamental grasses — qualities that make it remarkably adaptable across a wide range of residential landscape conditions. The sterile nature (no viable seed set) makes it entirely safe to plant near natural areas without invasive risk. Cut to 4–6 inches in late February before new growth emerges; it is otherwise essentially maintenance-free.
sources: Perennial Plant Association — Perennial Plant of the Year 2001

planting.note: Plant container roses in late winter through spring in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), fertile, loamy soil in full sun; The Fairy is one of the most adaptable and disease-resistant roses available and tolerates a wider range of soils and partial shade than most landscape roses.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; The Fairy is a classic polyantha rose introduced in 1932 by Ann Bentall that has remained continuously popular for nearly a century — a testament to its exceptional combination of qualities: outstanding disease resistance, long blooming season from late spring through hard frost, profuse clusters of small (1-inch), cupped, double, soft-pink flowers in dense panicles, and a compact, spreading mounding habit requiring minimal pruning. Where modern tea rose hybrids are high-maintenance plants demanding regular spraying for black spot and disease control, The Fairy is genuinely low-maintenance — rarely requiring fungicide treatment even in humid Gulf South climates — and blooms dependably season after season without the intensive care that classic hybrid teas demand. The small, button-like pink flowers are produced in large, dense clusters on arching stems, creating an effect reminiscent of a compact, pink-flowering hydrangea at peak bloom. The Fairy is widely used as a landscape rose, groundcover rose, and in container plantings where its naturally arching, cascading habit is particularly attractive. The mild, apple-and-rose fragrance is pleasant but light. In zone 9, The Fairy is essentially evergreen to semi-evergreen and blooms from March through December — one of the longest bloom seasons of any commonly grown rose.
